---
title: "1.5 Standards of Judgment"
---

# 1.5 Standards of Judgment

The claims of this book should be read under clear standards.

First, the aim is not to replace successful physics with rhetorical novelty. The aim is to test whether a different primitive starting point can simplify the conceptual architecture while preserving, recovering, or reinterpreting the structures that matter. M1 should therefore be judged not by whether it sounds different, but by whether it is more economical, more coherent, and more structurally illuminating.

Second, not all claims in the book are of the same kind. Some are intended as **derivations**. Some are **correspondences**, showing that the framework reproduces known structures in a stated regime. Some are **interpretations**, proposing how the formalism might best be understood. Some are still **speculative**, included because they seem promising enough to expose rather than hide. These distinctions matter, and the book should be read with them in mind.

Third, success in this project would mean more than internal elegance. A successful momentum-first framework would have to do at least four things. It would have to reduce the number of genuinely primitive assumptions. It would have to make the relation between its central concepts cleaner rather than murkier. It would have to recover known physics where it claims to do so, without relying on hidden imports. And it would have to make the overall structure feel more resonant with the actual geometry and behavior of the world.

Failure, meanwhile, would also be clear. M1 fails if it merely renames familiar structures, if its simplifications are cosmetic, if it must quietly assume what it claims to explain, or if the burden of correspondence becomes too heavy for the proposed primitive to bear.

That is the standard under which the framework should be read. The next section begins the test.
